spontaneous_remission_rate: 1.6-1.7% at 7 years on standard care (population-level cohorts UK and US)
best_intervention_remission_rate: 46-58% at 5 years post-bariatric surgery; up to 86% at 1 year with >15% total weight loss via intensive dietary intervention (DiRECT trial: 36% at 2 years)
gap_size: Very large
barrier_detail: Remission is biologically achievable and demonstrated in multiple large RCTs but almost never offered as the primary clinical strategy; default is indefinite pharmacotherapy escalation
last_soc_change: 2023 — ADA Standards of Care added formal remission definition (HbA1c <6.5% off all glucose-lowering meds 3+ months) and early GLP-1/SGLT2 use; prior first-line was metformin-only since ~2006
outdated_treatments: Sulfonylureas (hypoglycemia risk, weight gain, no CV benefit) remain widely prescribed despite GLP-1 and SGLT2 inhibitors showing CV and renal protection; insulin as early add-on before weight-loss intervention is attempted
adjunctive_emerging: Berberine (equivalent HbA1c lowering to metformin in some trials), time-restricted eating, very low carbohydrate diet, resistance training as primary intervention (not adjunct)
frontier_signal: Tirzepatide achieving 15-22% body weight loss with high remission rates; semaglutide 2.4mg SUSTAIN trials; islet cell encapsulation (Vertex VX-880, Phase 1/2)

spontaneous_remission_rate: Not well-characterized as population baseline; routinely framed as irreversible in clinical culture despite contrary trial evidence
best_intervention_remission_rate: Up to 93% had medications discontinued or reduced with intensive residential lifestyle intervention; DASH + weight loss reduced guideline-based medication indication from 51% to 18%; Lancet eClinicalMedicine 2024 meta-analysis confirms lifestyle superiority in motivated patients
gap_size: Large — primarily a framing and practice gap
barrier_detail: The 'requires lifelong medication' framing is contradicted by multiple trials when intensive lifestyle intervention is actually delivered; the gap is delivery infrastructure, not biology
last_soc_change: 2017 — ACC/AHA lowered treatment threshold from 140/90 to 130/80 mmHg (JNC 8 2014 had controversially raised threshold for over-60s to 150/90); first-line drug classes (ACE/ARB, CCB, thiazide) unchanged since early 2000s
outdated_treatments: Beta-blockers as first-line antihypertensive (no longer recommended in most guidelines for uncomplicated hypertension but still widely prescribed); high-dose diuretic monotherapy
adjunctive_emerging: DASH diet + sodium restriction (as primary, not adjunct); potassium supplementation (magnitude comparable to one antihypertensive); resistance training (BP reduction comparable to aerobic); device-based renal denervation (FDA-cleared 2023, Medtronic Symplicity)
frontier_signal: Renal denervation (Symplicity HTN-3 initially failed, SPYRAL HTN OFF-MED and RADIANCE-HTN SOLO showed 8-10 mmHg reduction); RNA interference therapy zilebesiran (single injection, 6-month BP lowering, Phase 3 underway)

spontaneous_remission_rate: 11% achieve drug-free remission (DFR) at median 74 months; most cohorts show 10-15% DFR ceiling
best_intervention_remission_rate: Early aggressive treat-to-target improves DFR rates modestly; some biologic-era cohorts reach 20-25% DFR; on-drug remission rates (DAS28 <2.6) reach 40-60% with modern biologics and JAK inhibitors
gap_size: Small for drug-free remission (biological ceiling is genuinely low); large gap between on-drug remission and drug-free remission
barrier_type: biological (DFR); practice (on-drug remission underachieved due to treatment inertia)
barrier_detail: Treat-to-target strategy is guideline-recommended but poorly implemented in routine care; many patients remain on inadequate therapy without escalation
last_soc_change: 2021 — ACR guidelines added JAK inhibitors with safety caveats; IL-6 inhibitors and IL-17 added to biologics ladder; treat-to-target explicitly endorsed; first-line (MTX) unchanged since 1990s
outdated_treatments: Hydroxychloroquine or sulfasalazine monotherapy as long-term maintenance (appropriate bridging, not curative); COX-2 inhibitors as primary disease management; gold salts (essentially abandoned but occasionally seen in older patients)
adjunctive_emerging: Low-dose naltrexone (LDN) emerging in case series; dietary interventions (Mediterranean diet reduces DAS28 scores); HBOT in early case reports; photobiomodulation for joint inflammation
frontier_signal: CAR-T cell therapy (case reports in Germany, 2023-2024 showing sustained drug-free remission); regulatory T-cell therapies; anti-CD20 + tolerogenic approaches for DFR induction

spontaneous_remission_rate: Relapse in 19-45% within 1 year of antidepressant discontinuation after remission; relapse risk ratio (off-medication vs on) falls from 3.69x at 2 months to 1.34x at 5 years
best_intervention_remission_rate: 55-80% remain relapse-free at 1 year after well-managed discontinuation; psychotherapy (CBT) achieves comparable remission with better durability; psilocybin-assisted therapy showing 29-71% response at 3 months in treatment-resistant cases
gap_size: Moderate; narrows substantially over time for maintenance medication question
barrier_detail: Guidelines push long maintenance durations partly because short-term relapse risk is high, but long-term maintenance evidence is weaker than commonly assumed; psychotherapy significantly underutilized relative to medication
last_soc_change: 2023 — FDA approved esketamine (Spravato) nasal spray for TRD; 2023 FDA approved psilocybin-assisted therapy pathway (Australian TGA 2023, US FDA Breakthrough designation); first-line SSRIs unchanged since early 1990s
outdated_treatments: Tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs) in routine first-line use; benzodiazepine co-prescribing as long-term adjunct (dependence risk, no antidepressant effect); antipsychotic augmentation before psychotherapy is attempted
adjunctive_emerging: Exercise (meta-analysis: comparable to SSRI for mild-moderate depression); phototherapy (for non-seasonal depression, underutilized); anti-inflammatory interventions (NSAIDs, omega-3) for inflammatory subtype; vagus nerve stimulation (FDA-cleared 2005, coverage finally added 2023)
frontier_signal: Psilocybin-assisted therapy (COMP360, FDA Breakthrough); MDMA-assisted therapy (FDA review ongoing); rapid-acting glutamatergic agents (ketamine analogs); transcranial magnetic stimulation (expanded protocols)

spontaneous_remission_rate: 60-85% achieve seizure freedom on medication; of those, 50-75% remain seizure-free after withdrawal (varies by syndrome and duration of freedom)
best_intervention_remission_rate: Not clearly higher than on-medication rates -- the real gap is behavioral/practice
gap_size: Small biological gap; large practice gap
barrier_detail: Only 26-45% of seizure-free patients who are candidates for medication withdrawal ever attempt it; physician and patient reluctance vastly exceeds biological risk
last_soc_change: 2023 — FDA approved cenobamate, fenfluramine for Dravet; first-line ASM (levetiracetam, lamotrigine, valproate) unchanged since 1990s-2000s; ketogenic diet formally endorsed in 2018 AES guidelines
outdated_treatments: Phenobarbital as first-line in adults (cognitive effects, dependence; still >50% of first-line therapy in low-income countries and some US rural settings); phenytoin loading in status epilepticus (fosphenytoin preferred); carbamazepine without therapeutic drug monitoring
adjunctive_emerging: Ketogenic diet (proven for drug-resistant epilepsy, underutilized); vagus nerve stimulation; responsive neurostimulation (RNS); low-glycemic index treatment; CBD (Epidiolex approved, broader CBD products under study)
frontier_signal: Gene therapy for SCN1A (Dravet syndrome) in early trials; antisense oligonucleotides (ASOs) for genetic epilepsies; optogenetic approaches; closed-loop neurostimulation with seizure prediction

spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous clearance in <30% of acute cases; chronic infection rarely clears without treatment (<1%/year)
best_intervention_remission_rate: >95% cured (sustained virologic response at 12 weeks) with 8-12 week courses of direct-acting antivirals (DAAs)
gap_size: Near-zero biological gap; large access/utilization gap
barrier_detail: Only 65% of eligible patients with private insurance received DAA treatment as of 2020; rates declining since 2019; Medicaid restrictions remain in many states; $20,000+ drug cost for generics unavailable in US
last_soc_change: 2014-2016 — DAA revolution (sofosbuvir 2013, ledipasvir/sofosbuvir 2014, pan-genotypic regimens 2016-2017); prior interferon-ribavirin standard unchanged since ~2001 with <50% efficacy and severe side effects
outdated_treatments: Interferon-based regimens (now essentially obsolete but occasionally prescribed in settings with limited DAA access); ribavirin-only maintenance (no antiviral effect alone)
adjunctive_emerging: Harm reduction integration (needle exchange + DAA treatment as combined strategy); primary-care-based treatment (task-shifting from hepatologist to GP dramatically increases uptake in trials)
frontier_signal: HCV vaccine candidates (multiple Phase 1/2, MRC/NIH); cure for HCV reactivation in immunocompromised; ultra-short (4-week) DAA regimens under investigation

spontaneous_remission_rate: Disease course unchanged in ~85% of patients at 10 years on standard care; ~23% (CD) and ~12% (UC) relapse within 12 months of thiopurine withdrawal in sustained remission
best_intervention_remission_rate: Anti-IL-23 biologics (mirikizumab, risankizumab) maintain steroid-free remission in >60% at 2 years — but on the drug, not drug-free
gap_size: Large -- but between 'no remission' and 'on-drug remission'; drug-free remission remains rare
barrier_detail: Biologic revolution has raised on-drug remission without meaningfully improving drug-free remission rates; true disease modification (cure-like durability off treatment) remains elusive
last_soc_change: 2023 — IL-23 inhibitors added to UC/CD ladder; 2018-2022 JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, upadacitinib) added; anti-TNF (infliximab) first-line since 1998, essentially unchanged as backbone
outdated_treatments: Long-term corticosteroids as maintenance (bone density loss, metabolic effects; guidelines explicitly recommend against steroid-dependent maintenance); azathioprine monotherapy without biologic in moderate-severe disease
adjunctive_emerging: Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) — RCT evidence in UC showing ~32% remission vs 9% placebo; exclusive enteral nutrition (EEN) in pediatric Crohn's comparable to steroids; low-FODMAP diet; exclusive dietary therapy trials underway
frontier_signal: FMT (multiple Phase 3 underway); stem cell transplantation for refractory CD (ASTIC trial); selective JAK1 inhibitors (filgotinib, upadacitinib); gut-selective S1P modulators; anti-TL1A (tulisokibart, Phase 3)

spontaneous_remission_rate: 26% of childhood-onset asthma in clinical remission by adulthood; <20% adult-onset achieves sustained remission; 65% long-term remission in mild-moderate vs 16% in severe
best_intervention_remission_rate: Biologics (anti-IL-5, anti-IL-4/13, anti-IgE) achieve near-zero exacerbation rates in eligible severe patients; allergen immunotherapy (AIT) achieves sustained drug-free remission in allergic asthma in ~30-40% of treated patients
gap_size: Moderate; highly severity-dependent; large practice gap for AIT
barrier_detail: Allergen immunotherapy is the only disease-modifying treatment for allergic asthma and is underused relative to guidelines; biological phenotyping (T2-high vs T2-low) drives outcomes more than treatment intensity alone
last_soc_change: 2022 — GINA guidelines introduced 'anti-inflammatory reliever' strategy (ICS-formoterol as reliever replacing SABA monotherapy); biologics formally endorsed as step 5 since ~2016; ICS as first-line unchanged since 1990s
outdated_treatments: Short-acting beta-agonist (SABA) monotherapy as reliever without ICS (GINA 2019 explicitly removed SABA-only from recommended options after evidence of harm); oral corticosteroid 'burst' as go-to for exacerbations without underlying biologic assessment
adjunctive_emerging: Bronchial thermoplasty (FDA approved, underutilized in severe non-biologic-eligible patients); vitamin D supplementation (meta-analysis: reduces exacerbation risk 50% in deficient patients); dietary intervention (Mediterranean diet reduces exacerbation frequency)
frontier_signal: Tezepelumab (anti-TSLP) for unselected severe asthma regardless of eosinophil count; dupilumab extended to children 6+ months; CRISPR approaches to IL-4 receptor editing in murine models

spontaneous_remission_rate: Effectively zero — airflow obstruction is irreversible by definition in COPD; FEV1 decline continues at 30-60 mL/year on standard care
best_intervention_remission_rate: Smoking cessation slows FEV1 decline to near-normal aging rate (the only intervention that does this); lung volume reduction surgery (LVRS) improves FEV1 15-20% in selected emphysema patients; 52% of COPD patients achieve clinically meaningful improvement after pulmonary rehabilitation
gap_size: Large for disease modification (smoking cessation the only real lever); moderate for functional outcomes (pulmonary rehab underused)
barrier_detail: Biological limit: structural lung damage is not reversible with any current pharmacotherapy. Practice gap: pulmonary rehabilitation achieves clinically meaningful improvement in 52% of patients but fewer than 2% of eligible COPD patients in the US receive it. 26% of patients with a COPD diagnosis have no evidence of airflow obstruction on spirometry (misdiagnosed, overtreated)
last_soc_change: 2024 — GOLD report added dupilumab for Type 2 inflammation subgroup (eosinophilic COPD); triple therapy (LABA/LAMA/ICS) endpoint data (IMPACT, ETHOS trials 2018-2020); LABA/LAMA dual bronchodilation as backbone unchanged since ~2006
outdated_treatments: ICS monotherapy without LABA/LAMA in non-eosinophilic COPD (increases pneumonia risk, no mortality benefit); mucolytic agents (e.g., NAC) at standard doses (high-dose emerging evidence, but standard dose RCTs largely negative); long-term oral corticosteroids in stable COPD
adjunctive_emerging: High-dose NAC (1800 mg/day vs standard 600 mg) showing FEV1 benefit in Chinese cohorts; endobronchial valve placement (FDA approved, Zephyr valve) for hyperinflation in selected patients; airway clearance devices
frontier_signal: Dupilumab in eosinophilic COPD (BOREAS/NOTUS trials positive, 2023-2024); tezepelumab Phase 3 in COPD; lung regeneration approaches; stem cell therapy (early Phase 1); senolytic therapy (targeting lung senescent cells — navitoclax, dasatinib+quercetin)

spontaneous_remission_rate: Symptom recurrence in 70-80% within 6 months of PPI discontinuation in erosive GERD; highly variable in non-erosive disease; ~20% of GERD is nonerosive, where acid suppression is less effective
best_intervention_remission_rate: Weight loss of >10% reduces symptoms in 65-81% of obese patients; laparoscopic anti-reflux surgery (Nissen fundoplication) achieves long-term symptom control in 85-92% and eliminates daily PPI need in majority; bariatric surgery in obese patients near-eliminates GERD
gap_size: Large for lifestyle/surgical approaches vs standard chronic PPI use
barrier_detail: AGA 2022 guidelines explicitly recommend PPI de-escalation or discontinuation in patients without erosive esophagitis or Barrett's — yet 84 million monthly Medicare PPI claims in 2019 suggest massive overprescription; most patients are never offered surgical or intensive lifestyle options
last_soc_change: 2022 — AGA Clinical Practice Update on PPI de-prescribing (explicit guidance to attempt discontinuation in low-risk patients); 2022 vonoprazan (P-CAB) approved as alternative; PPI-first strategy unchanged since 1989
outdated_treatments: Indefinite daily PPI in uncomplicated, nonerosive GERD (80% of GERD cases do not meet criteria for long-term PPI; 70-80% of GERD patients on PPIs have no documented appropriate indication); H2 blocker + PPI combination (additive acid suppression not demonstrated)
adjunctive_emerging: Alginate-based formulations (Gaviscon Advance) as effective as PPI for nonerosive GERD in some RCTs; LINX magnetic sphincter augmentation device (FDA approved 2012, underused); dietary modification (low-acid, low-fat, eliminating triggers) as primary rather than adjunct
frontier_signal: Transoral incisionless fundoplication (TIF) procedure — no incision, outpatient, durable at 5 years; Vonoprazan (P-CAB) for PPI-refractory cases; microbiome-targeted approaches for functional heartburn vs true GERD differentiation

spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous reversion to sinus rhythm occurs in ~50% of paroxysmal AF within 24-48 hours; persistent AF almost never self-terminates; rate-control (the dominant strategy) by definition keeps the patient in AF
best_intervention_remission_rate: Catheter ablation achieves freedom from AF in ~70-80% at 1 year (paroxysmal), 50-60% (persistent); ablation superior to antiarrhythmic drugs (52% vs 71% AF recurrence, CABANA trial); EAST-AFNET 4 showed early rhythm control reduced major adverse CV events vs rate control
gap_size: Large — rate control vs rhythm control debate has shifted but practice hasn't caught up
barrier_detail: Guidelines now support earlier rhythm control and ablation, but rate control with lifelong anticoagulation remains the dominant practice pattern; AF management is described as 'moving ahead of established guidelines' per ESC 2023
last_soc_change: 2023 — ESC guidelines restructured with new AF management acronym (AF-CARE); recommendation for early rhythm control strengthened (EAST-AFNET 4 data); 2014 ACC/AHA rate-vs-rhythm neutral stance increasingly outdated; first-line rate control drugs (beta-blockers, digoxin) unchanged since 1990s
outdated_treatments: Digoxin for rate control (increased mortality signal in observational data, narrow therapeutic window; still widely used); amiodarone as first-line antiarrhythmic in younger patients (thyroid, pulmonary, hepatic toxicity; should be last-resort); rate control as default before rhythm assessment in younger patients
adjunctive_emerging: Risk factor management as active AF treatment (weight loss, alcohol cessation, sleep apnea treatment — each reducing AF burden by 30-50%); cardiac rehabilitation post-ablation; omega-3 FA (STRENGTH and REDUCE-IT conflicting data)
frontier_signal: Pulsed field ablation (PFA) — non-thermal, cardiac-specific, reducing esophageal injury risk (CE marked 2021, FDA approved 2023); AI-guided ablation with real-time lesion assessment; gene therapy targeting connexin-43 remodeling

spontaneous_remission_rate: Structural progression is essentially universal over 10+ years without intervention; pain fluctuates but functional decline is the expected trajectory; no disease-modifying therapy exists as of 2024
best_intervention_remission_rate: No cure or disease modification available; total joint replacement (TJR) achieves 85-90% patient satisfaction and near-elimination of pain but is not disease reversal; weight loss of >10% reduces pain scores ~50% and slows structural progression; exercise therapy comparable to NSAIDs for pain relief
gap_size: Large for pain management vs disease modification (disease modification doesn't exist); moderate for functional outcomes (exercise and weight loss underutilized vs medication)
barrier_detail: OARSI formally petitioned the FDA in 2016 to recognize OA as a serious disease with no cure and no disease-modifying interventions — confirmed in FDA's criteria. Practice gap: NSAIDs have been first-line for >100 years despite long-term evidence of joint damage acceleration and GI/CV risk; exercise consistently outperforms medication in functional outcomes but is rarely 'prescribed'
last_soc_change: 2019-2020 — OARSI/ACR guidelines formally de-emphasized acetaminophen (evidence of minimal efficacy) and added conditional recommendation against opioids; NSAIDs have been first-line since the 1960s; duloxetine added ~2010 as centrally-acting option
outdated_treatments: Long-term oral NSAID use (4-year MRI data shows NSAIDs may accelerate cartilage loss; GI bleed and CV risk substantial in elderly OA population); intra-articular corticosteroid injections repeated >3x/year (joint damage acceleration); opioids for chronic OA pain (mortality signal in observational data); acetaminophen (not meaningfully better than placebo in recent RCTs for OA pain)
adjunctive_emerging: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injections — multiple RCTs showing 3-6 month pain reduction; low-level laser therapy (LLLT); hydrogen sulfide donors (early animal data); senolytic therapy (dasatinib + quercetin — first human OA trial published 2019 showing reduced senescent cells)
frontier_signal: Lorecivivint (Wnt pathway inhibitor, Phase 3 failed 2022 but variant study ongoing); sprifermin (FGF-18, cartilage anabolic, Phase 2 positive); anti-nerve growth factor (tanezumab — FDA rejected over joint safety, remains contested); gene therapy (AAV-based IGF-1 delivery to joint)

spontaneous_remission_rate: Progressive and irreversible by definition; MMSE decline 1.07-3.4 points/year untreated; no spontaneous remission
best_intervention_remission_rate: Cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) slow MMSE decline to 0.2-1.37 points/year — slowing, not stopping; lecanemab (anti-amyloid) reduced clinical decline rate by 27% at 18 months in early AD (CLARITY AD, 2022); donanemab showed 35% slowing in amyloid/tau positive group
gap_size: Small for symptom slowing; biological ceiling is genuinely low with current approaches
barrier_detail: True disease reversal or cure is not achievable with any current therapy; cholinesterase inhibitors have consistent but modest effects (average 2.7-point ADAS-Cog improvement vs placebo); anti-amyloid biologics represent a genuine mechanistic step forward but are not cures and have significant side effects (ARIA — amyloid-related imaging abnormalities in 21-36%)
last_soc_change: 2023 — FDA full approval of lecanemab (Leqembi) for early AD — first disease-modifying therapy; 2003 memantine added; cholinesterase inhibitors approved 1993-2001; aducanumab (2021) withdrawn after controversy; this represents the first genuine SOC change since early 2000s
outdated_treatments: Vitamin E supplementation (early trials positive, replication failed; still widely used); ginkgo biloba (multiple large RCTs negative, including GuidAge 5-year trial); NSAIDs as preventive or therapeutic (consistently negative in trials); huperzine A (widespread but no Phase 3 evidence)
adjunctive_emerging: Metabolic/lifestyle intervention (FINGER, MAPT, PREDIVA trials — multimodal interventions show 30% reduction in cognitive decline); ketogenic diet (emerging mechanistic rationale and early trials); photobiomodulation for cognitive function (small RCTs, Phase 2 active); methylene blue (low-dose, Phase 2)
frontier_signal: Lecanemab and donanemab (first approved disease-modifying therapies); tau-targeting antibodies (semorinemab, zagotenemab Phase 3); GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide for AD, multiple Phase 3 including EVOKE trial); antisense oligonucleotides (APOE-targeting); ultrasound-mediated blood-brain barrier opening for drug delivery

spontaneous_remission_rate: Hashimoto's (most common cause) is irreversible in majority; spontaneous recovery in <5%; subclinical hypothyroidism spontaneously normalizes in ~30-40% of cases within 2 years without treatment
best_intervention_remission_rate: T4 monotherapy normalizes TSH in nearly all patients and resolves symptoms in ~60%; up to 40% of T4-treated patients remain symptomatic despite normalized TSH; T4+T3 combination or desiccated thyroid extract (DTE) preferred by ~50% of patients in blinded preference trials
gap_size: Moderate — within the treated population, a large minority are inadequately treated by the standard-of-care T4 monotherapy approach
barrier_detail: Up to 40% of patients remain symptomatic on T4 despite guideline-defined adequate treatment; T3 combination and DTE are rarely offered in primary care despite guideline acknowledgment that a trial may be appropriate; TSH-only monitoring misses tissue-level hypothyroidism
last_soc_change: 2014 — ATA guidelines last updated; acknowledged T4/T3 combination as appropriate trial in symptomatic patients; 2019 ETA/BTA guidelines more explicitly acknowledged need for personalized approach; T4 monotherapy has been standard since 1980s displacement of DTE
outdated_treatments: TSH-only monitoring without free T4 and T3 measurement in symptomatic patients; rigid adherence to T4 monotherapy in symptomatic patients without considering combination or DTE; desiccated thyroid extract (DTE) was the only treatment for 110+ years before being discarded largely for convenience reasons rather than evidence of inferiority
adjunctive_emerging: Selenium supplementation (reduces anti-TPO antibodies in Hashimoto's, may slow progression); low-dose naltrexone (LDN) in autoimmune thyroid disease; vitamin D optimization; gluten-free diet in Hashimoto's with coexistent celiac or non-celiac gluten sensitivity
frontier_signal: Thyroid regeneration via stem cells (early animal models); mRNA-based thyroid hormone delivery; immune tolerization for Hashimoto's prevention; thyroid-stimulating hormone receptor antibody (TRAb) targeted therapy for Graves' disease

spontaneous_remission_rate: ~90% of acute LBP episodes resolve within 6 weeks regardless of treatment; chronic LBP (>3 months) shows only 30-40% recovery at 1 year and high recurrence; opioid prescribing correlates inversely with recovery
best_intervention_remission_rate: Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) + graded exercise: 50-70% sustained improvement at 1 year in chronic LBP; Pain Reprocessing Therapy (PRT) — 66% pain-free or nearly pain-free at 1 year (JAMA Psychiatry 2021, vs 20% placebo); early active rehabilitation (vs bed rest) dramatically reduces chronification
gap_size: Large for psychologically-informed approaches vs current opioid/surgical dominant model
barrier_detail: Opioid prescribing for chronic LBP is associated with worse long-term outcomes yet remains extremely common; spinal fusion surgery rates 40x higher in US than UK despite no superiority over conservative care in most indications; active rehabilitation demonstrably superior to passive treatment but requires patient effort and provider time
last_soc_change: 2017 — ACP guidelines explicitly recommended against opioids as first-line for chronic LBP; 2016 CDC opioid prescribing guidelines; imaging-guided approach deprioritized; exercise and CBT as first-line since 2017 — but implementation is poor
outdated_treatments: Opioids as first-line or long-term treatment (CDC guidelines explicitly against this; long-term opioid therapy correlates with worse functional outcomes in LBP); routine lumbar spinal fusion for nonspecific chronic LBP (RCTs show no superiority over intensive rehabilitation); prolonged bed rest (explicitly contraindicated since 1990s, yet still advised by some practitioners); routine NSAID long-term use without reassessment
adjunctive_emerging: Pain Reprocessing Therapy (PRT) — 66% near-pain-free at 1 year; mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR) — RCT evidence; intradiscal biologics (platelet-rich plasma, stem cells — Phase 2 trials); spinal cord stimulation for failed back surgery syndrome
frontier_signal: Pain Reprocessing Therapy (growing RCT base); resiniferatoxin (ultra-potent TRPV1 agonist causing ablation of pain-signaling neurons — Phase 1/2); biologic disc regeneration (GDF-5, TGF-beta intradiscal injection Phase 2); dorsal root ganglion stimulation (FDA approved 2016 for CRPS, expanding to LBP)

spontaneous_remission_rate: Migraine frequency fluctuates; spontaneous remission (no attacks for 12+ months) occurs in ~25% over 10-year follow-up; 40% of migraine sufferers qualify for preventive therapy but only 3-13% receive it
best_intervention_remission_rate: CGRP monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab) achieve ≥50% reduction in monthly migraine days in 50-70% of patients; complete response (near-zero days) in 10-30%; acute triptans achieve 2-hour pain freedom in 30-40%
gap_size: Large — primarily an access/utilization gap for preventive therapy
barrier_detail: Only 3-13% of the ~10 million US adults who qualify for preventive therapy receive it; prior authorization barriers for CGRP mAbs are substantial; widespread underdiagnosis; opioid prescribing for migraine in ED associated with medication overuse headache
last_soc_change: 2018-2020 — FDA approved first CGRP-targeted preventive therapies (erenumab 2018, fremanezumab/galcanezumab 2018-2019, atogepant 2021); prior prevention (beta-blockers, topiramate, amitriptyline) unchanged since 1990s-2000s; triptans first-line acute since 1993
outdated_treatments: Opioids for acute migraine (associated with medication overuse headache, MOH, in 50-70% of frequent users; actually worsens long-term migraine frequency); opioids in ED for acute migraine (inferior to IV prochlorperazine + diphenhydramine, yet remain common); barbiturate-containing analgesics (Fioricet/butalbital — MOH risk, no superiority over NSAIDs)
adjunctive_emerging: Magnesium IV/oral (effective for acute migraine and prevention; dramatically underused); riboflavin 400mg/day (preventive; NNT ~3 in RCTs); CoQ10 300mg/day (Cochrane-reviewed); neuromodulation devices (Cefaly transcranial supraorbital stimulation, FDA cleared; gammaCore vagus nerve stimulation)
frontier_signal: Gepants (oral CGRP receptor antagonists — ubrogepant, rimegepant — acute and preventive dual role); lasmiditan (5-HT1F agonist without vasoconstriction, for cardiovascular-contraindicated patients); psilocybin for cluster headache (NIH-funded Phase 2 underway); transcranial magnetic stimulation (SpringTMS, FDA cleared)

spontaneous_remission_rate: Untreated relapsing MS: ~1 relapse/year; disability accumulates over time in ~85% who convert to secondary progressive MS; spontaneous remission from MS is not possible
best_intervention_remission_rate: High-efficacy DMTs (natalizumab, alemtuzumab, ocrelizumab, cladribine) achieve 0 clinical relapses in 50-70% at 2 years and >95% MRI lesion suppression; early high-efficacy treatment shows superior outcomes vs platform therapy escalation
gap_size: Large — massive gap between platform DMT (interferons, glatiramer) and high-efficacy DMT outcomes, but practice still favors escalation model
barrier_detail: Treat-to-target with early high-efficacy therapy is superior to the traditional safety-first escalation model (TREAT-MS, DELIVER-MS trials) but most MS neurologists still start with platform therapies; hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) achieves 70-80% no-evidence-of-disease-activity (NEDA) at 5 years but is rarely offered
last_soc_change: 2023 — FDA approved tolebrutinib and fenebrutinib (BTK inhibitors) for primary progressive MS; ocrelizumab approved for PPMS 2017 (first ever treatment); platform therapies (interferons, glatiramer) unchanged since 1993-1996; paradigm shift to high-efficacy first gaining guideline endorsement only now
outdated_treatments: Escalation approach starting with interferon-beta or glatiramer acetate despite evidence that early high-efficacy treatment substantially reduces long-term disability accumulation; repeated IV corticosteroid use for frequent relapses without reassessing disease-modifying therapy adequacy
adjunctive_emerging: Biotin (high-dose, 100-300mg/day) in progressive MS — Phase 3 equivocal but ongoing; vitamin D optimization (strong epidemiological association, RCT evidence modest); intermittent fasting/ketogenic diet for neuroprotection (animal models; Phase 2 underway); exercise as neuroprotective intervention
frontier_signal: BTK inhibitors (tolebrutinib, fenebrutinib, evobrutinib) for progressive MS — target CNS-resident microglia; remyelination strategies (opicinumab, PIPE-307); HSCT (MIST trial showed superiority over mitoxantrone; Phase 3 vs alemtuzumab underway); CAR-T cell therapies in early trials

spontaneous_remission_rate: MASH (formerly NASH) with fibrosis progresses to cirrhosis in ~20% over 10 years on standard care (which until 2024 was lifestyle modification only); fibrosis regression occurs spontaneously in ~25% over 5 years with sustained weight loss
best_intervention_remission_rate: Resmetirom (Rezdiffra) — first FDA-approved pharmacotherapy for MASH (March 2024): 26-30% histological resolution at 52 weeks vs 10% placebo; bariatric surgery achieves MASH resolution in 85% and fibrosis regression in 35% at 5 years; >7-10% weight loss resolves MASH in ~50%
gap_size: Large — first drug only just approved in 2024 after decades of failed trials; practice still dominated by lifestyle advice alone
barrier_detail: Until March 2024, there was literally no approved pharmacotherapy for MASH despite it being a leading cause of cirrhosis and liver transplant; the field repeatedly failed Phase 3 (obeticholic acid, elafibranor, selonsertib, cenicriviroc — all negative); resmetirom is a genuine breakthrough but access and prescribing uptake are still ramping up
last_soc_change: March 2024 — resmetirom (Rezdiffra) FDA approved — the first pharmacotherapy for MASH; prior standard was weight loss and lifestyle only since MASH was described in 1980
outdated_treatments: Vitamin E (early RCT positive in non-diabetic NASH — PIVENS trial; subsequent use became widespread but long-term safety concerns emerged; not guideline-recommended in diabetics or cirrhosis); metformin (repeatedly negative in MASH RCTs despite glucose benefit); omega-3 fatty acids at standard doses (Phase 3 negative for histology despite lipid benefit)
adjunctive_emerging: Coffee consumption (3+ cups/day associated with 40% reduced fibrosis progression — not a supplement, but a genuine finding); prebiotic/probiotic interventions; SGLT2 inhibitors (secondary benefit on liver histology in MASH — RCT data emerging); exercise as primary therapy (reduces liver fat independently of weight loss)
frontier_signal: Resmetirom (just approved); semaglutide for MASH (Phase 3 ESSENCE trial — histological resolution 63% vs 34%; results 2024); lanifibranor (PPAR-alpha/delta/gamma, Phase 3 NATIVE trial positive); combination approaches; FGF21 analogs (pegbelfermin); FXR agonists next generation

spontaneous_remission_rate: GFR decline averages 2-5 mL/min/year on standard care (ACE/ARB + blood pressure control); progression to end-stage renal disease (ESRD) in ~20-30% at 10 years for moderate CKD; reversal essentially impossible once structural fibrosis established
best_intervention_remission_rate: SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) reduce progression to ESRD by 30-35% in CKD trials (EMPA-KIDNEY, DAPA-CKD); finerenone (non-steroidal MRA) adds 15-20% additional protection; combination slows but does not reverse disease
gap_size: Moderate — SGLT2 inhibitors represent a genuine paradigm shift but are dramatically underused
barrier_detail: SGLT2 inhibitors have robust evidence for CKD protection regardless of diabetes status (EMPA-KIDNEY 2023) but prescribing rates in non-diabetic CKD remain very low; ACE/ARB as sole strategy (established in 1990s-2000s) still dominant despite the new data
last_soc_change: 2023 — KDIGO guidelines updated to include SGLT2 inhibitors as first-line in CKD regardless of diabetes (EMPA-KIDNEY data); finerenone added for CKD in T2DM; ACE/ARB first-line unchanged since 2000; this is the first major CKD pharmacotherapy advance in 20 years
outdated_treatments: ACE/ARB monotherapy without SGLT2 inhibitor in eligible CKD patients (KDIGO now recommends SGLT2 inhibitor as co-first-line); protein restriction beyond moderate levels (evidence equivocal, high compliance burden); erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs) to high hemoglobin targets (increased CV risk — TREAT and CHOIR trials; target now 10-11 g/dL, not 13+)
adjunctive_emerging: SGLT2 inhibitors (just becoming standard, but still underused); low-protein plant-predominant diet (PLADO trial — reduces hyperfiltration, lower acid load); bicarbonate supplementation (slows GFR decline in acidotic CKD — small but real effect); bardoxolone methyl (NRF2 activator, Phase 3 CARDINAL trial positive for CKD in Alport syndrome)
frontier_signal: Sparsentan (dual endothelin/angiotensin antagonist — FDA approved for IgA nephropathy 2023); avacopan (complement inhibitor for ANCA vasculitis CKD); atrasentan (selective endothelin-A, Phase 3 ZENITH trial positive for IgA nephropathy 2024); kidney organoid-based replacement therapy (5-10 year horizon)

spontaneous_remission_rate: Essentially zero for established T1DM (honeymoon phase occurs in 50-60% after diagnosis but lasts 3-12 months then ends); the disease is permanent autoimmune destruction of beta cells
best_intervention_remission_rate: Closed-loop insulin delivery (artificial pancreas systems — Control-IQ, Omnipod 5) achieves 70-80% time-in-range vs 60% with standard therapy; teplizumab (anti-CD3 mAb) delays T1DM onset by 2+ years in at-risk individuals; islet cell transplantation achieves insulin independence in ~44% at 5 years (CITR data)
gap_size: Moderate-large — closed-loop approaches are increasingly standard but true insulin independence is rare with any available therapy
barrier_detail: Beta cell regeneration and transplantation face immune rejection barriers (all islet recipients require chronic immunosuppression); closed-loop systems dramatically improve quality of life and glycemic control but require intensive engagement and are expensive; teplizumab (FDA approved 2022) for delay is not a cure
last_soc_change: 2022 — teplizumab (Tzield) FDA approved for Stage 2 T1DM (first immunotherapy to delay progression); 2023 closed-loop systems widely recommended; 2021 Omnipod 5 cleared; ADA continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) recommended for all T1DM 2020; insulin unchanged as backbone since 1921
outdated_treatments: Multiple daily injection (MDI) with finger-stick monitoring in patients who would benefit from CGM + closed-loop (evidence clearly superior but access barriers remain); fixed insulin regimens without carbohydrate counting; NPH insulin use in settings where insulin analogues are available
adjunctive_emerging: SGLT2 inhibitors as adjunct in T1DM (off-label, DKA risk with protocol; FDA rejected for T1D but widely used with mitigation strategies); GLP-1 agonists as adjunct (weight and dose reduction); ketogenic diet (very low-carb significantly reduces insulin requirements and variability)
frontier_signal: Vertex VX-880 (stem-cell derived islet cells — Phase 1/2, insulin independence achieved in treated patients 2023); teplizumab for prevention in at-risk individuals; anti-thymocyte globulin (ATG) + GCSF for beta cell preservation; encapsulated islet transplantation (no immunosuppression needed — Phase 1 underway)
